skill.name: senior-secops
skill.description: Review application security posture, vulnerability handling, compliance checks, and secure development practices with lightweight SecOps guidance and local reporting scripts.
skill.summary: SecOps guidance and report scripts for security reviews, vulnerability triage, and compliance checks.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high-risk patterns, but manual review found the backtick, weak-crypto, environment, and reconnaissance matches are documentation or placeholder false positives. The Python scripts perform local path validation and can write JSON reports to a user-supplied output path. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, credential access, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled JSON Report Output Path
low_findings.description: Each Python script accepts --output and writes JSON results to that path. This is expected CLI behavior, but users should avoid sensitive paths because an existing file could be overwritten.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write operation is direct and confirmed in all three scripts. Risk is limited because the output path is explicitly supplied by the user and no hidden write target was found.

low_findings.title: Documentation Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are Markdown fenced examples and usage instructions. They do not execute commands by themselves and contain no dynamic shell construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are visible Markdown code fences. No Ruby backtick operator or runtime shell execution was found in these locations.

low_findings.title: Environment File Setup Mention Is Not Secret Access
low_findings.description: The environment-related finding points to a setup command that copies .env.example to .env. No file read, credential parsing, or secret exfiltration is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is a documentation example for local setup. I found no code that opens .env files or sends environment values elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Matches Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static high-risk weak-crypto and reconnaissance matches point to generic prose, headings, and argparse descriptions. No cryptographic algorithm use, system probing, or host enumeration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of each referenced area found placeholder documentation or parser metadata. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, weak ciphers, uname calls, host scans, or similar behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled JSON Report Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: Each Python script accepts --output and writes JSON results to that path. This is expected CLI behavior, but users should avoid sensitive paths because an existing file could be overwritten.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operation is direct and confirmed in all three scripts. Risk is limited because the output path is explicitly supplied by the user and no hidden write target was found.

content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss repeatable structure and clear reporting. This skill gives teams guided SecOps workflows and local report scripts for reviews, triage, and compliance checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides application security reviews using the included SecOps workflow documentation.
Runs local placeholder scripts for security scanning, vulnerability assessment, and compliance checks.
Validates that a target path exists before analysis starts.
Prints a concise local report with target, status, and finding count.
Can emit JSON results to standard output or a user-selected file.
Provides reference material for security standards, vulnerability workflows, and compliance requirements.
limitations: The included scripts are scaffolds and do not perform deep vulnerability detection.
Compliance guidance is generic and must be mapped to each organization.
The scripts do not authenticate to scanners, clouds, ticketing tools, or SIEM platforms.
Report output paths are user controlled and can overwrite files.

use_cases.title: Prepare a security review checklist
use_cases.description: Create a structured review plan for application security, vulnerability triage, and compliance evidence collection.

use_cases.title: Standardize engineering audit handoffs
use_cases.description: Use the local scripts and references to produce a consistent report outline for engineering teams.

use_cases.title: Document compliance review steps
use_cases.description: Turn generic compliance requirements into a practical task list for product and platform teams.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small project security review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this project for basic SecOps concerns. Focus on authentication, input validation, dependencies, secrets, and reportable next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Triage vulnerability findings
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request after receiving scanner or dependency results.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me triage these vulnerability findings. Group them by severity, exploitability, affected component, owner, and remediation priority.

prompt_templates.title: Map controls to compliance needs
prompt_templates.prompt: Map this application architecture to security and compliance controls. Identify evidence, control gaps, and owner actions for each area.

prompt_templates.title: Build a SecOps remediation plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a cross-team remediation program.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a SecOps remediation plan from these risks. Include priorities, validation steps, reporting cadence, acceptance criteria, and residual risk notes.

output_examples.input: Assess a web service before an internal security review.
output: A prioritized review checklist covering authentication, authorization, secrets, dependencies, logging, and deployment controls.
A short risk summary with owners, recommended fixes, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: Triage dependency alerts for a product team.
output: A severity-ranked vulnerability list with business impact and suggested remediation order.
A follow-up plan for patch testing, release tracking, and closure evidence.

output_examples.input: Prepare compliance evidence for a release gate.
output: A control checklist with required evidence, missing artifacts, and owners.
A release decision summary that separates blockers from follow-up tasks.

best_practices: Run the scripts only against paths you own or are authorized to review.
Review generated JSON before sharing it because target paths may reveal project details.
Pair the generic guidance with your own control framework and scanner results.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the scaffold scripts as a complete vulnerability scanner.
Do not write output files into sensitive directories or production paths.
Do not accept compliance conclusions without human review and evidence validation.

faq.question: Does this skill run a real vulnerability scanner?
faq.answer: No. The included scripts are lightweight scaffolds that validate paths and produce simple local reports.

faq.question: Can it help with compliance audits?
faq.answer: Yes. It can structure control reviews and evidence planning, but a qualified reviewer must validate final conclusions.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence of network calls or external data transfer was found during review.

faq.question: What files can the scripts write?
faq.answer: They can write JSON output to the file path supplied with the --output option.

faq.question: Is this safe for sensitive repositories?
faq.answer: Use care. The scripts run locally, but reports may include target paths and should be reviewed before sharing.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The skill declares support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
